content_en:
The Conquest of Mecca (8 AH / 630 CE) is the most dramatic and consequential military event of the Prophet's ﷺ career — and one of the most remarkable conquests in human history, distinguished not by the scale of the battle (there was almost none) but by the extraordinary mercy shown by the conquering force and the completeness of the transformation it achieved.

The Hudaybiyyah treaty had included a clause prohibiting military aggression by either party. The Quraysh violated this treaty when their allied tribe, the Banu Bakr, attacked the Banu Khuza'ah — allies of the Muslims — with Qurayshi material support. This gave the Prophet ﷺ legitimate grounds to march. He assembled an army of ten thousand — the largest Muslim force yet assembled — and marched toward Mecca with such operational secrecy that the Quraysh learned of his approach only when the army was virtually at their gates.

Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, the de facto leader of Mecca's resistance, rode out in the night and encountered the Muslim advance force. Through a series of events involving Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib (the Prophet's ﷺ uncle), Abu Sufyan was brought before the Prophet ﷺ in his camp. The Prophet ﷺ offered him a declaration: 'Woe to you, Abu Sufyan! Is it not time you knew there is no god except Allah?' Abu Sufyan's resistance had already crumbled psychologically; he accepted Islam. The Prophet ﷺ then granted him a special status: Abu Sufyan's house in Mecca would be declared a sanctuary for any Meccan who took refuge there. This act of political generosity toward a former enemy served both to honor Abu Sufyan's conversion and to give the Meccans a face-saving path to submission.

The army entered Mecca from four directions simultaneously. The Prophet ﷺ entered from the south, bowed in profound humility on his camel, reciting the verse: 'Indeed, We have given you a clear conquest' (Quran 48:1). There was minimal resistance — only the columns under Khalid ibn al-Walid's command encountered hostile fighters, in a brief engagement in which approximately twelve men were killed.

Upon entering the Kaaba, the Prophet ﷺ found it filled with 360 idols. He went to each one and struck it with his staff, reciting: 'Truth has come and falsehood has departed; indeed, falsehood was bound to depart' (Quran 17:81). The idols were removed and destroyed, and the Kaaba was restored to its Abrahamic purpose as the house of the worship of Allah alone — the culmination of a prophetic mission that had begun twenty years earlier with a single man in a cave on a mountain outside this very city.

The most anticipated moment was the Prophet's ﷺ address to the assembled Meccans — including many who had spent years persecuting him and his Companions. He asked them: 'What do you think I will do to you?' They replied: 'Good, noble brother, son of a noble brother.' He declared: 'Go — you are free.' This act of unconditional general amnesty (al-'afwa al-'am) for the Meccan population — with only a small number of specified exceptions — stands as one of the great acts of magnanimity in human history. Thousands of Meccans accepted Islam that day. The city that had rejected and persecuted the Prophet ﷺ became the holiest city in Islam.
